title: Introduction of Morse code
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Morse code is a telecommunications method which encodes text characters as standardized sequences of two different signal durations, called dots and dashes, or dits and dahs.

title: Introduction of Hangul
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Korean alphabet is the modern writing system for the Korean language.

title: Introduction of Cyrillic script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Cyrillic script is a writing system used for various languages across Eurasia.

title: Introduction of Egyptian hieroglyphs
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Ancient Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language.

title: Introduction of cuneiform
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Cuneiform is a logo-syllabic writing system that was used to write several languages of the ancient Near East.

title: Introduction of Devanagari
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Devanagari is an Indic script used in the Indian subcontinent.

title: Introduction of Chinese characters
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Chinese characters are logographs used to write the Chinese languages and others from regions historically influenced by Chinese culture, including Japan, Korea, and Vietnam.

title: Introduction of runic script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Runes are the letters in a set of related alphabets, known as runic rows, runic alphabets or futharks, native to the Germanic peoples.

title: Introduction of Latin script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Latin script, also known as the Roman script, is a writing system based on the letters of the classical Latin alphabet, derived from a form of the Greek alphabet which was in use in the ancient Greek city of Cumae in Magna Graecia.

title: Introduction of leet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Leet, also known as eleet, leetspeak, or simply hacker speech, is a system of modified spellings used primarily on the Internet.

title: Introduction of Bangla alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Bengali script or Bangla alphabet is the standard writing system used to write the Bengali language, and has historically been used to write Sanskrit within Bengal.

title: Introduction of Linear B
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Linear B is a syllabic script that was used for writing in Mycenaean Greek, the earliest attested form of the Greek language.

title: Introduction of Braille script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Braille is a tactile writing system used by blind or visually impaired people.

title: Introduction of Elder Futhark
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Elder Futhark, or Pan-Germanic Futhark, is the oldest form of the runic alphabets.

title: Introduction of Arabic script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Arabic script is the writing system used for Arabic and several other languages of Asia and Africa.

title: Introduction of Helvetica
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Helvetica, also known by its original name Neue Haas Grotesk, is a widely used sans-serif typeface developed in 1957 by Swiss typeface designer Max Miedinger and Eduard Hoffmann.

title: Introduction of Ogham
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Ogham is an Early Medieval alphabet used primarily to write the early Irish language, and later the Old Irish language.

title: Introduction of Japanese writing system
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The modern Japanese writing system uses a combination of logographic kanji, which are adopted Chinese characters, and syllabic kana.

title: Introduction of Hanja
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Hanja, alternatively spelled Hancha, are Chinese characters used to write the Korean language.

title: Introduction of Armenian alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Armenian alphabet or, more broadly, the Armenian script, is an alphabetic writing system developed for Armenian and occasionally used to write other languages.

title: Introduction of Brahmi
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Brahmi is a writing system from ancient India that appeared as a fully developed script in the 3rd century BCE.

title: Introduction of Aramaic alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The ancient Aramaic alphabet was used to write the Aramaic languages spoken by ancient Aramean pre-Christian peoples throughout the Fertile Crescent.

title: Introduction of Linear A
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Linear A is a writing system that was used by the Minoans of Crete from 1800 BC to 1450 BC.

title: Introduction of Times New Roman
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Times New Roman is a serif typeface commissioned for use by the British newspaper The Times in 1931.

title: Introduction of Mongolian
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The traditional Mongolian script, also known as the Hudum Mongol bichig, was the first writing system created specifically for the Mongolian language, and was the most widespread until the introduction of Cyrillic in 1946.

title: Introduction of Georgian scripts
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Georgian scripts are the three writing systems used to write the Georgian language: Asomtavruli, Nuskhuri and Mkhedruli.

title: Introduction of Proto-Sinaitic
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Proto-Sinaitic script is a Middle Bronze Age writing system known from a small corpus of about 30–40 inscriptions and fragments from Serabit el-Khadim in the Sinai Peninsula, as well as two inscriptions from Wadi el-Hol in Middle Egypt.

title: Introduction of Fraktur
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Fraktur is a calligraphic hand of the Latin alphabet and any of several blackletter typefaces derived from this hand.

title: Introduction of kana
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Kana are syllabaries used to write Japanese phonological units, morae.

title: Introduction of Geʽez script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Geʽez is an abugida used to write several Afro-Asiatic and Nilo-Saharan languages of Ethiopia and Eritrea.

title: Introduction of Tifinagh
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Tifinagh is a script used to write the Berber languages.

title: Introduction of Serbian Cyrillic alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Serbian Cyrillic alphabet, also known as the Serbian script, is a standardized variation of the Cyrillic script used to write Serbo-Croatian.

title: Introduction of Mayan hieroglyphs
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Maya script, also known as Maya glyphs, is historically the native writing system of the Maya civilization of Mesoamerica and is the only Mesoamerican writing system that has been substantially deciphered.

title: Introduction of Tamil script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Tamil script is an abugida script that is used by Tamils and Tamil speakers in India, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, Singapore and elsewhere to write the Tamil language.

title: Introduction of Indus script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Indus script, also known as the Harappan script and the Indus Valley script, is a corpus of symbols produced by the Indus Valley Civilisation.

title: Introduction of oracle bone script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Oracle bone script is the oldest attested form of written Chinese, dating to the late 2nd millennium BC.

title: Introduction of Glagolitic
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Glagolitic script is the oldest known Slavic alphabet.

title: Introduction of Gurmukhi
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Gurmukhī is an abugida developed from the Laṇḍā scripts, standardized and used by the second Sikh guru, Guru Angad (1504–1552).

title: Introduction of Arial
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Arial is a sans-serif typeface in the neo-grotesque style.

title: Introduction of Tengwar
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Tengwar script is an artificial script, one of several scripts created by J.

title: Introduction of Tibetan alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Tibetan script is a segmental writing system, or abugida, forming a part of the Brahmic scripts, and used to write certain Tibetic languages, including Tibetan, Dzongkha, Sikkimese, Ladakhi, Jirel and Balti.

title: Introduction of Syriac alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Syriac alphabet is a writing system primarily used to write the Syriac language since the 1st century.

title: Introduction of Futura
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Futura is a geometric sans-serif typeface designed by Paul Renner and released in 1927.

title: Introduction of Cherokee syllabary
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Cherokee syllabary is a syllabary invented by Sequoyah in the late 1810s and early 1820s to write the Cherokee language.

title: Introduction of Wade-Giles
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Wade–Giles is a romanization system for Mandarin Chinese.

title: Introduction of Kannada script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Kannada script is an abugida of the Brahmic family, used to write Kannada, one of the Dravidian languages of South India especially in the state of Karnataka.

title: Introduction of Rongorongo
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Rongorongo is a system of glyphs discovered in the 19th century on Easter Island that has the appearance of writing or proto-writing.

title: Introduction of Early Cyrillic alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Early Cyrillic alphabet, also called classical Cyrillic or paleo-Cyrillic, is an alphabetic writing system that was developed in Bulgaria in the Preslav Literary School during the late 9th century.

title: Introduction of Canadian Aboriginal syllabics
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Canadian syllabic writing, or simply syllabics, is a family of writing systems used with a number of indigenous Canadian languages of the Algonquian, Eskaleut, and (formerly) Athabaskan language families.

title: Introduction of Frutiger
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Frutiger is a series of typefaces named after its Swiss designer, Adrian Frutiger.

title: Introduction of Sütterlin
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Sütterlinschrift is the last widely used form of Kurrent, the historical form of German handwriting script that evolved alongside German blackletter typefaces.

title: Introduction of Garamond
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Garamond is a group of many serif typefaces, named for sixteenth-century Parisian engraver Claude Garamond, generally spelled as Garamont in his lifetime.

title: Introduction of Egyptian hieratic
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Hieratic is the name given to a cursive writing system used for Ancient Egyptian and the principal script used to write that language from its development in the third millennium BCE until the rise of Demotic in the mid-first millennium BCE.

title: Introduction of Gothic script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Gothic alphabet is an alphabet for writing the Gothic language.

title: Introduction of Egyptian Demotic
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Demotic is the ancient Egyptian script derived from northern forms of hieratic used in the Nile Delta.

title: Introduction of Pe̍h-ōe-jī
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Pe̍h-ōe-jī, also known as Church Romanization, is an orthography used to write variants of Hokkien Southern Min, particularly Taiwanese and Amoy Hokkien.

title: Introduction of Gujarati
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Gujarati script is an abugida for the Gujarati language, Kutchi language, and various other languages.

title: Introduction of Avestan
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Avestan alphabet is a writing system developed during Iran's Sasanian era (226–651 CE) to render the Avestan language.

title: Introduction of Calibri
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Calibri is a digital sans-serif typeface family in the humanist or modern style.

title: Introduction of seal script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Seal script or sigillary script is a style of writing Chinese characters that was common throughout the latter half of the 1st millennium BC.

title: Introduction of Gill Sans
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Gill Sans is a humanist sans-serif typeface designed by Eric Gill and released by the British branch of Monotype in 1928.

title: Introduction of Old South Arabian script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Ancient South Arabian script branched from the Proto-Sinaitic script in about the late 2nd millennium BCE, and remained in use through the late sixth century CE.

title: Introduction of Shavian alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Shavian alphabet is a constructed alphabet conceived as a way to provide simple, phonemic orthography for the English language to replace the inefficiencies and difficulties of conventional spelling using the Latin alphabet.

title: Introduction of Sinhala script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Sinhalese script, also known as Sinhala script, is a writing system used by the Sinhalese people and most Sri Lankans in Sri Lanka and elsewhere to write the Sinhalese language as well as the liturgical languages Pali and Sanskrit.

title: Introduction of Kawi script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Kawi script or the Old Javanese script is a historic Brahmic script used across Maritime Southeast Asia between the 8th century and the 16th century.

title: Introduction of Etruscan alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Etruscan alphabet was used by the Etruscans, an ancient civilization of central and northern Italy, to write their language, from about 700 BC to sometime around 100 AD.

title: Introduction of Courier
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Courier is a monospaced slab serif typeface commissioned by IBM and designed by Howard "Bud" Kettler (1919–1999) in the mid-1950s.

title: Introduction of N’ko
subtitle: Writing system
fact: N'Ko (ߒߞߏ), also spelled Nko, is an alphabetic script devised by Solomana Kanté in 1949, as a modern writing system for the Manding languages of West Africa.

title: Introduction of Kharoṣṭhī
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Kharosthi script, also known as the Gandhari script, was an ancient Indic script originally developed in the Gandhara Region of the north-western Indian subcontinent, between the 5th and 3rd century BCE.

title: Introduction of Manchu alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Manchu alphabet is the alphabet used to write the now critically endangered Manchu language.

title: Introduction of Cretan hieroglyphs
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Cretan hieroglyphs are a hieroglyphic writing system used in early Bronze Age Crete, during the Minoan era.

title: Introduction of man'yōgana
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Man'yōgana is an ancient writing system that uses Chinese characters to represent the Japanese language.

title: Introduction of Ugaritic alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Ugaritic alphabet is an abjad with syllabic elements written using the same tools as cuneiform, which emerged c. 1400 or 1300 BCE to write Ugaritic, an extinct Northwest Semitic language; it fell out of use amid the Late Bronze Age collapse c.

title: Introduction of Odia
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Odia script is a Brahmic script, mainly used to write the Odia language.

title: Introduction of Pallava
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Pallava script, or Pallava Grantha, named after the Pallava dynasty of Southern India (Tamilakam) and is attested to since the 4th century CE.

title: Introduction of Sogdian
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Sogdian alphabet was originally used for the Sogdian language, a language in the Iranian family used by the people of Sogdia.

title: Introduction of Palatino
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Palatino is an old-style serif typeface designed by Hermann Zapf, initially released in 1949 by the Stempel foundry and later by other companies, most notably the Mergenthaler Linotype Company.

title: Introduction of Vinča symbols
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Vinča symbols are a set of undeciphered symbols found on artifacts from the Neolithic Vinča culture and other "Old European" cultures of Central and Southeast Europe.

title: Introduction of Kaithi
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Kaithi, also called Kayathi, Kayasthi, Kayastani, or Kaite Lipi in Nepali, is a Brahmic script historically used across parts of Northern and Eastern India.

title: Introduction of ʼPhags-pa
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Phagspa, ʼPhags-pa or ḥPʻags-pa script is an alphabet designed by the Tibetan monk and State Preceptor Drogön Chögyal Phagpa (1235–1280) for Kublai Khan, the founder of the Yuan dynasty (1271–1368) in China, as a unified script for the written languages within the Yuan.

title: Introduction of Bodoni
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Bodoni is the name given to the serif typefaces first designed by Giambattista Bodoni (1740–1813) in the late eighteenth century and frequently revived since.

title: Introduction of Ol Chiki
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Ol Chiki script, also known as Ol Chemetʼ, Ol Ciki, Ol, and Santali alphabet is the official writing system for Santali, an Austroasiatic language recognized as an official regional language in India.

title: Introduction of Siddhaṃ
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Siddhaṃ is an Indic script used in India from the 6th century to the 13th century.

title: Introduction of Samaritan alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Samaritan Hebrew script, or simply Samaritan script, is the alphabet used by the Samaritans for their religious and liturgical writings.

title: Introduction of Yi script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Yi scripts are two scripts used to write the Yi languages; Classical Yi, and the later Yi syllabary.

title: Introduction of clerical script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The clerical script, sometimes also chancery script, is a style of Chinese writing that evolved from the late Warring States period to the Qin dynasty.

title: Introduction of Verdana
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Verdana is a humanist sans-serif typeface designed by Matthew Carter for Microsoft, with hand-hinting done by Thomas Rickner, then at Monotype.

title: Introduction of Gupta
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Gupta script was used for writing Sanskrit and is associated with the Gupta Empire of the Indian subcontinent, which was a period of material prosperity and great religious and scientific developments.

title: Introduction of Grantha
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Grantha script is a classical South Indian Brahmic script, found particularly in Tamil Nadu and Kerala.

title: Introduction of Sharada
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Śāradā script is an abugida writing system of the Brahmic family of scripts.

title: Introduction of Cypriot syllabary
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Cypriot or Cypriote syllabary is a syllabic script used in Iron Age Cyprus, from about the 11th to the 4th centuries BCE, when it was replaced by the Greek alphabet.

title: Introduction of Bangla–Assamese script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Bengali–Assamese script, sometimes also known as Eastern Nagri, is an eastern Brahmic script, primarily used today for the Bengali and Assamese language spoken in eastern South Asia.

title: Introduction of Modi
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Modi is an old script used to write the Marathi language, which is the primary language spoken in the state of Maharashtra, India.

title: Introduction of Nabataean script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Nabataean script is an abjad that was used to write Nabataean Aramaic and Nabataean Arabic from the second century BC onwards.

title: Introduction of Blissymbols
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Blissymbols or Blissymbolics is a constructed language conceived as an ideographic writing system called Semantography consisting of several hundred basic symbols, each representing a concept, which can be composed together to generate new symbols that represent new concepts.

title: Introduction of Anatolian Hieroglyphs
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Anatolian hieroglyphs are an indigenous logographic script native to central Anatolia, consisting of some 500 signs.

title: Introduction of DejaVu fonts
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The DejaVu fonts are a superfamily of fonts designed for broad coverage of the Unicode Universal Character Set.

title: Introduction of Nāgarī
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Nāgarī script is the ancestor of Devanagari, Nandinagari and other variants, and was first used to write Prakrit and Sanskrit.

title: Introduction of Belarusian Arabic alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Belarusian Arabic alphabet or the Belarusian Arabica was based on the Perso-Arabic script and was developed in the 15th or 16th century.

title: Introduction of Old Permic alphabet
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Old Permic script, sometimes known by its initial two characters as Abur or Anbur, is a "highly idiosyncratic adaptation" of the Cyrillic script once used to write medieval Komi.

title: Introduction of Tahoma
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Tahoma is a humanist sans-serif typeface that Matthew Carter designed for Microsoft.

title: Introduction of Osmanya
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Osmanya, known in Somali as Far Soomaali and in Arabic as al-kitābah al-ʿuthmānīyah, is an alphabetic script created to transcribe the Somali language.

title: Introduction of Yañalif
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The New Turkic Alphabet, known in Turkic languages as Yañalif, is the first Latin alphabet used during the Latinisation in the Soviet Union in the 1930s for the Turkic languages.

title: Introduction of Soyombo
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Soyombo script is an abugida invented by the Mongolian Buddhist monk Zanabazar in 1686 to write Mongolian.

title: Introduction of Clear Script
subtitle: Writing system
fact: The Clear Script is an alphabet created in 1648 by the Oirat Lamaist monk Zaya Pandita for the Oirat language.

title: Introduction of Antiqua
subtitle: Writing system
fact: Antiqua is a style of typeface used to mimic styles of handwriting or calligraphy common during the 15th and 16th centuries.
